questions.question: State and prove the Basic Pigeonhole Principle.
keyPoints: Statement: If n items are placed into m containers and n > m, then at least one container has more than one item.
Proof by contradiction: Assume each container has at most one item. Then maximum items = m. But we placed n > m items. Contradiction!
Therefore, at least one container must contain at least 2 items.

questions.question: Explain the Generalized Pigeonhole Principle with an example.
keyPoints: Statement: If n items are placed into m containers, then at least one container contains ⌈n/m⌉ items.
Example: In a class of 50 students with 12 months, ⌈50/12⌉ = 5 students share birth month.
Proof uses division algorithm: n = qm + r where 0 ≤ r < m, then q+1 = ⌈n/m⌉.

questions.question: Prove that in any group of 6 people, there are either 3 who all know each other or 3 who are all strangers to each other.
keyPoints: This is R(3,3) = 6, the smallest number guaranteeing monochromatic triangle.
Consider one person A. Among 5 other people, A knows at least 3 or doesn't know at least 3 (by pigeonhole).
Case 1: A knows 3 people. If any two of them know each other, we have triangle of mutual acquaintances. If none know each other, those 3 are mutual strangers.
Case 2: A doesn't know 3 people. Similar argument yields either triangle of mutual strangers or all 3 know each other.
Therefore, 6 people guarantee either 3 mutual friends or 3 mutual strangers.

questions.question: Find the minimum number of integers to be selected from {1,2,...,50} to guarantee two numbers whose sum is 51.
keyPoints: Pairs that sum to 51: (1,50), (2,49), ..., (25,26) - total 25 disjoint pairs.
If we select 26 numbers, by pigeonhole principle (26 > 25), we must select at least one complete pair.
Proof: In worst case, we could pick one from each pair = 25 numbers with no sum 51.
The 26th number must pair with an existing one to form sum 51.

questions.question: Apply Pigeonhole Principle to show that in any sequence of n²+1 distinct integers, there is either an increasing subsequence of length n+1 or a decreasing subsequence of length n+1.
keyPoints: This is the Erdos-Szekeres theorem.
For each element, define a pair (a_i, d_i) where a_i = length of longest increasing subsequence ending at element i, d_i = length of longest decreasing subsequence ending at element i.
All pairs are distinct because sequence has no duplicates.
If there's no increasing sequence of length n+1, then a_i ≤ n for all i. Similarly for decreasing.
By pigeonhole: With n²+1 elements and only n² possible pairs (1 to n), two elements must have same pair values. This leads to contradiction, proving the theorem.

questions.question: Using Pigeonhole Principle, prove that any comparison-based sorting algorithm requires at least Ω(n log n) comparisons in the worst case. Explain the connection to information theory.
keyPoints: There are n! possible permutations of n elements that a sorting algorithm must distinguish.
Each comparison yields binary information (YES/NO), forming a decision tree.
To distinguish n! permutations, decision tree must have at least n! leaves.
A binary tree with L leaves has height at least log₂ L.
Therefore, worst-case comparisons ≥ log₂(n!)
Using Stirling's approximation: log₂(n!) ≈ n log₂ n - 1.44n = Ω(n log n).
This lower bound proves optimality of algorithms like Merge Sort and Heap Sort.

questions.question: Explain the Birthday Paradox. Show that in a group of just 23 people, the probability of at least two sharing a birthday exceeds 50%. Generalize this for any number n.
keyPoints: Birthday paradox: Intuition suggests need 183 people for >50% chance (365/2). But actually only 23 needed!
Calculation: Probability no two share birthday = (365/365) × (364/365) × ... × (343/365) for 23 people.
This equals approximately 0.493, so probability of sharing ≥ 0.507 > 50%.
Why? Because we check 253 pairs (C(23,2)), not individual comparisons. 253 > 365/2 triggers pigeonhole-style probability crossing.
Generalization: For n days and k people, probability of no shared birthday ≈ e^(-k(k-1)/(2n)).
Set probability < 0.5: k(k-1) > 2n × ln(2), approximately k ≈ √(2n ln 2). For n=365, k≈22.5.

questions.question: Design a hash table with collision resolution and explain how Pigeonhole Principle guarantees collisions when load factor exceeds 1. Discuss practical implications for system design.
keyPoints: Hash table with m slots storing n items has load factor α = n/m.
If n > m (α > 1), by pigeonhole principle, collision is guaranteed.
This is fundamental to hash table design: keep α < 1 (typically 0.5-0.75) for performance.
Collision resolution techniques: Open addressing (linear probing, quadratic probing, double hashing) and Chaining.
Pigeonhole principle also applies to: Bloom filters (false positives inevitable), birthday attack in cryptography, birthday paradox in randomized algorithms.
System design implications: Network routing (pigeonholes=routers), CPU scheduling, memory allocation all face this fundamental limitation.

questions.question: State and prove Dirichlet's Approximation Theorem. How is it related to Pigeonhole Principle?
keyPoints: Dirichlet's Theorem: For any real number α and positive integer N, there exist integers p,q with 1 ≤ q ≤ N such that |α - p/q| < 1/(qN) ≤ 1/q².
Proof using pigeonhole: Consider fractional parts {0·α}, {1·α}, ..., {N·α}. Divide [0,1) into N equal intervals (pigeonholes).
With N+1 numbers (pigeons), two fractional parts fall in same interval: |iα - jα| < 1/N for i≠j.
Let q = |i-j|, then |qα - p| < 1/N for some integer p = ⌊qα⌋.
Thus |α - p/q| < 1/(qN) ≤ 1/q².
This connects number theory to pigeonhole principle - the irrational spacing of multiples of α guarantees approximation.
